steps.explanation: Substitute $(x+2)$ for $x$ in $f$. Since $g(x) = f(x+2)$ and $f(x) = 9(4)^x$: $g(x) = 9(4)^{x+2}$.

Evaluate all choices:
  • Choice A: $18(4)^x$. This would come from $9 \cdot 2 = 18$, not $9 \cdot 16$. Incorrect.
  • Choice B: $144(4)^x$. This equals $9 \cdot 16 \cdot (4)^x$, exactly what we derived. That matches choice B — B is correct.
  • Choice C: $18(8)^x$. The base 8 would require adding the bases, not multiplying exponents. Incorrect.
  • Choice D: $81(16)^x$. This misapplies the shift by squaring the coefficient ($9^2 = 81$) and using 16 as the new base. Incorrect.
